title: M/s Infosys BPM Limited v. Rajesh Kumar Sharma
court: High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru
keyArguments: The employee was terminated for cause following a documented internal inquiry that established willful misconduct and repeated violations of the company's code of conduct.
The domestic inquiry was conducted in accordance with the principles of natural justice, with adequate notice and opportunity to present a defence provided to the employee at every stage.
The employee's claim of wrongful termination was contradicted by his own signed acknowledgment of the code of conduct and two prior written warnings on record.
No evidence was produced by the employee to establish malafide intent or victimisation on the part of the employer, and the termination was proportionate to the gravity of the misconduct found.
The Labour Court below had exceeded its jurisdiction by substituting its own view on quantum of punishment without finding the inquiry vitiated or the findings perverse.
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Karnataka set aside the order of the Labour Court which had directed reinstatement of the respondent-employee with back wages. The Court held that the domestic inquiry was fair and that the findings of misconduct were supported by evidence on record. The employer's right to impose the penalty of dismissal was upheld, and the Labour Court's interference was found to be unwarranted in the absence of any perversity in the inquiry findings.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA AT BENGALURU

Writ Petition No. 14892 of 2022

BETWEEN:
M/s Infosys BPM Limited, having its registered office at Electronics City, Bengaluru - 560 100, represented by its Head of Human Resources ... Petitioner

AND:
Rajesh Kumar Sharma, S/o Late Mohan Lal Sharma, aged about 34 years, residing at No. 47, 3rd Cross, Jayanagar, Bengaluru - 560 041 ... Respondent

This Writ Petition is filed under Articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India praying to quash the Award dated 18.03.2022 passed by the Presiding Officer, IV Additional Labour Court, Bengaluru in I.D. No. 233 of 2019.

Date of Judgment: 14th February 2023

JUDGMENT

The petitioner-company challenges the Award of the IV Additional Labour Court, Bengaluru whereby the termination of the respondent-employee was set aside and reinstatement with fifty percent back wages was ordered.

Brief facts as they emerge from the record are that the respondent was employed as a Senior Process Executive with the petitioner company since 2014. In the year 2018, upon receipt of complaints from two colleagues alleging data misappropriation and unauthorised access to client accounts, the petitioner initiated a domestic inquiry. A charge-sheet was issued on 15.06.2018, to which the respondent submitted a reply. An Inquiry Officer was appointed, and proceedings were conducted on five dates between July and September 2018. The respondent was represented by a co-worker of his choice. The Inquiry Officer found the charges of unauthorised access to client data and sharing of confidential information with an external party to be substantively proved. On the basis of the inquiry report, the disciplinary authority passed an order of dismissal on 12.11.2018. The respondent raised an industrial dispute which was referred to the Labour Court. The Labour Court, while not specifically finding the inquiry to be vitiated, held that the punishment of dismissal was disproportionate and directed reinstatement with back wages.

Having heard learned counsel for both parties and perused the materials on record, this Court is of the considered opinion that the Labour Court committed a manifest error of law. It is settled law, as enunciated by the Supreme Court in Bharat Forge Co. Ltd. v. Uttam Manohar Nakate, (2005) 2 SCC 489, and reiterated in a series of subsequent decisions, that where a domestic inquiry has been held to be fair and the findings are not perverse, the Labour Court or Industrial Tribunal cannot sit in appeal over the findings of the Inquiry Officer or substitute a lesser punishment merely because it would have taken a different view. The jurisdiction of the Labour Court under Section 11-A of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, while permitting interference with the quantum of punishment, does not authorise mechanical reduction of penalty without cogent recorded reasons.

In the present case, the Labour Court recorded no finding that the inquiry was procedurally defective or that the conclusions of the Inquiry Officer were unsupported by evidence. The finding of the Labour Court that dismissal was disproportionate was based on sympathy and not on any legal or factual foundation. The respondent, a Senior Process Executive handling sensitive client data, was found to have committed a serious breach of trust. Two prior written warnings had been issued to him for related infractions. The misconduct fell squarely within the category of acts warranting dismissal as per the Standing Orders applicable to the establishment.

This Court finds that the impugned Award is unsustainable in law. The Writ Petition is accordingly allowed. The Award dated 18.03.2022 passed by the IV Additional Labour Court, Bengaluru in I.D. No. 233 of 2019 is hereby quashed. The order of dismissal dated 12.11.2018 passed by the petitioner-company is restored. No order as to costs.

Sd/-
HONOURABLE MR. JUSTICE K. VENKATARAMAN
Judge
